name: Dick Johnson
abstract: Richard Brown Johnson (December 1, 1925 – January 10, 2010) was an American big band clarinetist, best known for his work with the Artie Shaw Band. From 1983 until his death he was the leader of the Artie Shaw Orchestra.

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: Mel Torme with Herb Pomeroy Big Band and Gerry Mulligan/ Gerry Mulligan Sextet
Carnegie Hall Jazz Band: Artie Shaw and Woody Herman
concerts.venue: Isaac Stern Auditorium / Ronald O. Perelman Stage

works.t: Flying Down to Rio: The Carioca
The New Moon: Softly, as in a Morning Sunrise

about_quotes.speaker: Jake Hanna
about_quotes.evidence: But the band I was with was Dave on piano and Dick Johnson on alto
about_quotes.doc_title: Jake Hanna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Clem Raymond
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee only responds to the interviewer's mention of Dick Johnson with 'He's some kind of Jellie Roll.' There is no indication of a personal relationship, collaboration, or direct interaction between the interviewee and Dick Johnson.

about_quotes.speaker: Jeff Riddick
about_quotes.evidence: So Dick Johnson asked me, does he play? So he says, come on and sit in and play a couple of tunes. So I played three tunes and sang black and blue.

about_quotes.speaker: Ken Peplowski
about_quotes.evidence: Dick used to play in Buddy Morrow’s band in the early 1950s... But he did a lot for me. So that was my big job experience.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ken Peplowski interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 10, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Milt Fillius
about_quotes.evidence: He picked Dick Johnson to do the clarinet... I was down there one night and I went to hear them.
about_quotes.doc_title: Milt Fillius interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 18, 2000

about_quotes.speaker: Sidney Desvigne
about_quotes.evidence: Do you remember Dick Johnson's place over there? Never did. I don't remember his place.
